     assert_array_equal(res, np.array(expected, dtype=str_dt))
 
 
+@pytest.mark.parametrize("str_dt", "US")
+@pytest.mark.parametrize("size", [-1, np.iinfo(np.intc).max])
+def test_string_size_dtype_errors(str_dt, size):
+    if size > 0:
+        size = size // np.dtype(f"{str_dt}1").itemsize + 1
+
+    with pytest.raises(ValueError):
+        np.dtype((str_dt, size))
+    with pytest.raises(TypeError):
+        np.dtype(f"{str_dt}{size}")
+
+
+@pytest.mark.parametrize("str_dt", "US")
+def test_string_size_dtype_large_repr(str_dt):
+    size = np.iinfo(np.intc).max // np.dtype(f"{str_dt}1").itemsize
+    size_str = str(size)
+
+    dtype = np.dtype((str_dt, size))
+    assert size_str in dtype.str
+    assert size_str in str(dtype)
+    assert size_str in repr(dtype)
+
+
+@pytest.mark.slow
+@requires_memory(2 * np.iinfo(np.intc).max)
+@pytest.mark.parametrize("str_dt", "US")
+def test_large_string_coercion_error(str_dt):
+    very_large = np.iinfo(np.intc).max // np.dtype(f"{str_dt}1").itemsize
+    try:
+        large_string = "A" * (very_large + 1)
+    except Exception:
+        # We may not be able to create this Python string on 32bit.

#27786 and re-do of #27859.

Thanks to @seiko2plus for the suggestion to convert this code to C++ and use C++17 features. Let's see if any of the platforms we run CI on object to using this, it passes the tests on my Macbook Pro.

There is an earlier commit included in this PR that keeps things in C and uses the CPython-internal `_PyRWMutex`, which also seems to work, but using C++ lets us avoid the need to write a PEP or making argument to the C API workgroup that making `_PyRWMutex` public would be nice for C extensions.

#27786 and re-do of #27859.

Thanks to @seiko2plus for the suggestion to convert this code to C++ and use C++17 features. Let's see if any of the platforms we run CI on object to using this, it passes the tests on my Macbook Pro.

There is an earlier commit included in this PR that keeps things in C and uses the CPython-internal `_PyRWMutex`, which also seems to work, but using C++ lets us avoid the need to write a PEP or make an argument to the C API workgroup that making `_PyRWMutex` public would be nice for C extensions.

With the test script in the issue, I see the following scaling using `std::shared_mutex`:

#27786 and re-do of #27859.

Thanks to @seiko2plus for the suggestion to convert this code to C++ and use C++17 features. Let's see if any of the platforms we run CI on object to using this, it passes the tests on my Macbook Pro.

There is an earlier commit included in this PR that keeps things in C and uses the CPython-internal `_PyRWMutex`, which also seems to work, but using C++ lets us avoid the need to write a PEP or make an argument to the C API workgroup that making `_PyRWMutex` public would be nice for C extensions.

With the test script in the issue, I see the following scaling using `std::shared_mutex`:

`pytest-timeout` sets a per-test timeout that also prints a C stack trace if it kills a timed out test runner. It looks like a few years ago there were issues using pytest-timeout and pytest-xdist together, but they seem to have been fixed.

I added it to `test-requirements.txt` since it's a pure-python dependency and should be harmless even if it's not needed. I can also just `pip install` it in the wheel testing script if that would be preferred.

I'm passing `--durations` and `--timeout` for all the wheel builds because it seems useful to me to always have this information and to always timeout if a single test takes longer than 30 minutes. I can also just do it for the free-threaded build if reviewers would prefer only doing it is an attempt to track down the hang reported in #27872.

`pytest-timeout` sets a per-test timeout that also prints a C stack trace if it kills a timed out test runner. It looks like a few years ago there were issues using pytest-timeout and pytest-xdist together, but they seem to have been fixed.

I added it to `test-requirements.txt` since it's a pure-python dependency and should be harmless even if it's not needed. I can also just `pip install` it in the wheel testing script if that would be preferred.

I'm passing `--durations` and `--timeout` for all the wheel builds because it seems useful to me to always have this information and to always timeout if a single test takes longer than 30 minutes. I can also just do it for the free-threaded build if reviewers would prefer only doing it is an attempt to track down the hang reported in #27872.

`pytest-timeout` sets a per-test timeout that also prints a C stack trace if it kills a timed out test runner. It looks like a few years ago there were issues using pytest-timeout and pytest-xdist together, but they seem to have been fixed.

I added it to `test-requirements.txt` since it's a pure-python dependency and should be harmless even if it's not needed. I can also just `pip install` it in the wheel testing script if that would be preferred.

I'm passing `--durations` and `--timeout` for all the wheel builds because it seems useful to me to always have this information and to always timeout if a single test takes longer than 30 minutes. I can also just do it for the free-threaded build if reviewers would prefer only doing it #27786 and re-do of #27859.

Thanks to @seiko2plus for the suggestion to convert this code to C++ and use C++17 features. Let's see if any of the platforms we run CI on object to using this, it passes the tests on my Macbook Pro.

There is an earlier commit included in this PR that keeps things in C and uses the CPython-internal `_PyRWMutex`, which also seems to work, but using C++ lets us avoid the need to write a PEP or make an argument to the C API workgroup that making `_PyRWMutex` public would be nice for C extensions.

With the test script in the issue, I see the following scaling using `std::shared_mutex`:

#27786 and re-do of #27859.

Thanks to @seiko2plus for the suggestion to convert this code to C++ and use C++17 features. Let's see if any of the platforms we run CI on object to using this, it passes the tests on my Macbook Pro.

There is an earlier commit included in this PR that keeps things in C and uses the CPython-internal `_PyRWMutex`, which also seems to work, but using C++ lets us avoid the need to write a PEP or make an argument to the C API workgroup that making `_PyRWMutex` public would be nice for C extensions.

With the test script in the issue, I see the following scaling using `std::shared_mutex`:

Unfortunately then we run into the issue of finding a portable implementation. My brief research indicates that there isn't a standardized shared recursive mutex because of the performance trade-offs, although the posix shared read-write lock is recursive.

If someone does try to use this reentrantly, it will deadlock. Do you think that's problematic in practice?

Thanks for the pointer about exceptions. I found [`std::nothrow`](https://en.cppreference.com/w/cpp/memory/new/nothrow) which seems to be doing what you wanted with a little less boilerplate - is that OK? Testing","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2024-12-03T19:58:17Z","updated_at":"2024-12-04T18:44:04Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"MEMBER","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/pulls/27899","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/27899","diff_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/27899.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/27899.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"This is an attempt to track down the hang reported in #27872.

`pytest-timeout` sets a per-test timeout that also prints a C stack trace if it kills a timed out test runner. It looks like a few years ago there were issues using pytest-timeout and pytest-xdist together, but they seem to have been fixed.

I added it to `test-requirements.txt` since it's a pure-python dependency and should be harmless even if it's not needed. I can also just `pip install` it in the wheel testing script if that would be preferred.

I'm passing `--durations` and `--timeout` for all the wheel builds because it seems useful to me to always have this information and to always timeout if a single test takes longer than 30 minutes. I can also just do it for the free-threaded build if reviewers would prefer only doing it there.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27899/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27899/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2518258914","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/27899#issuecomment-2518258914","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27899","id":2518258914,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAA3dP86WGZTi","user":{"login":"ngoldbaum","id":3126246,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjMxMjYyNDY=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/3126246?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/ngoldbaum","html_url":"https://github.com/ngoldbaum","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ngoldbaum/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ngoldbaum/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ngoldbaum/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ngoldbaum/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ngoldbaum/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ngoldbaum/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ngoldbaum/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ngoldbaum/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ngoldbaum/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-12-04T18:44:02Z","updated_at":"2024-12-04T18:44:02Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"IMO it's worth having these timeouts for heisenbugs and other infrequent hangs. `pytest-timeout` sets a per-test timeout that also prints a C stack trace if it kills a timed out test runner. It looks like a few years ago there were issues using pytest-timeout and pytest-xdist together, but they seem to have been fixed.

I added it to `test-requirements.txt` since it's a pure-python dependency and should be harmless even if it's not needed. I can also just `pip install` it in the wheel testing script if that would be preferred.

I'm passing `--durations` and `--timeout` for all the wheel builds because it seems useful to me to always have this information and to always timeout if a single test takes longer than 30 minutes. I can also just do it for the free-threaded build if reviewers would prefer only doing it I also took a look at contributors and saw the maintaining is almost exclusive and there is no documentation related to a developer who wants to see which part of your codes are the core, which are modules , how do you manage match operation, where to begin to understand . this is absolutely the opposite of open-source philosophy , and as a programmer, I can tell you there is no one who can come in to this repository and figure it out even a bit !! why ??? is there any hidden documentation ? come on , me and a friend of mine have checked this repo , and even my teacher , no one could understand what is this . no one has told what to do to figure this out .

wish you luck and bests .

Question","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":"Question about NumPy usage or development"}],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2024-12-08T01:37:56Z","updated_at":"2024-12-08T17:03:07Z","closed_at":"2024-12-08T17:03:07Z","author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Describe the issue:

I'm trying to install numpy for another repo [freqtrade](https://github.com/freqtrade/freqtrade). It requires numpy v1.26.4, and I'm in the process of verifying the issue on a more recent release v2.1.3.

I'm trying to set up a system to do trading stuff on an old computer, namely a system with a Pentium Pro CPU and 128 MB of RAM. The features of the CPU are extremely limited since the Pentium Pro has NO SIMD extensions. I found a page on your support forum (https://numpy.org/doc/2.1/reference/simd/build-options.html) about compiling numpy for a host system and tried to do that namely:

```
python -m build --wheel -Csetup-args=-Dcpu-baseline="native" -Csetup-args=-Dcpu-dispatch="none"
```

To which I can get the build system to skip trying to detect SIMD extensions, since for some reason it still finds them even though they aren't supported by my processor? I think that may be a different bug wrt the `detect` system detecting unsupported features.

Here is the output of `cat /proc/cpuinfo`
```
processor	: 0
vendor_id	: GenuineIntel
cpu family	: 6
model		: 1
model name	: Pentium Pro
stepping	: 7
microcode	: 0xc6
cpu MHz		: 199.429
cache size	: 256 KB
physical id	: 0
siblings	: 1
core id		: 0
cpu cores	: 1
apicid		: 0
initial apicid	: 0
fdiv_bug	: no
f00f_bug	: no
coma_bug	: no
fpu		: yes
fpu_exception	: yes
cpuid level	: 2
wp		: yes
flags		: fpu vme de pse tsc msr pae mce cx8 mtrr pge mca cmov cpuid pti
bugs		: cpu_meltdown spectre_v1 spectre_v2 spec_store_bypass l1tf mds swapgs itlb_multihit mmio_unknown
bogomips	: 398.85
clflush size	: 32
cache_alignment	: 32
address sizes	: 36 bits physical, 32 bits virtual
power management:

```

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
wget https://files.pythonhosted.org/packages/source/n/numpy/numpy-1.26.4.tar.gz
tar -xvf ./numpy-1.26.4.tar.gz
cd numpy-1.26.4
python -m build --wheel -Csetup-args=-Dcpu-baseline="native" -Csetup-args=-Dcpu-dispatch="none"
```


### Error message:

```shell
[272/311] Generating numpy/random/_bounded_integer_pxd with a custom command
FAILED: numpy/random/_bounded_integers.pxd 
/tmp/build-env-72buuq3_/bin/python /home/aaron/workspace/numpy-build/numpy-1.26.4/numpy/_build_utils/tempita.py ../numpy/random/_bounded_integers.pxd.in -o numpy/random/_bounded_integers.pxd
Illegal instruction
[273/311] Compiling C object numpy/linalg/_umath_linalg.cpython-311-i386-linux-gnu.so.p/lapack_lite_f2c_blas.c.o
ninja: build stopped: subcommand failed.

ERROR Backend subprocess exited when trying to invoke build_wheel
```


### Python and NumPy Versions:

python: 3.11.2
numpy: 1.26.4

### Runtime Environment:

I created a virtual environment with the following packages installed:
```
meson 
ninja 
cython==0.29.34  # this is important as newer versions of cython contain illegal instructions as well
build
```

### Context for the issue:

It doesn't really block my work, as I have other systems, but I feel that it should be important that numpy can be compiled without SIMD extensions.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27929/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27929/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":"not_planned"}}

Enhancement","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":8,"created_at":"2024-11-18T17:20:30Z","updated_at":"2024-12-08T22:50:51Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/pulls/27789","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/27789","diff_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/27789.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/27789.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"This commit adds a `np.strings.slice` function which vectorizes slicing of string arrays. For example
```
    >>> a = np.array(['hello', 'world'])
    >>> np.char.slice(a, 2)
    array(['he', 'wo'], dtype='<U5')
```

This supports fixed-length and variable-length string dtypes. It also supports broadcasting the start, stop, step args:
```
    >>> b = np.array(['hello world', 'γεια σου κόσμε', '你好世界', '👋 🌍️'], dtype=np.dtypes.StringDType())
    >>> np.strings.slice(b, [3, 0, 2, 1], -1)
    array(['lo worl', 'γεια σου κόσμ', '世', ' 🌍'], dtype=StringDType())
```

Closes #8579

Enhancement","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":4,"created_at":"2024-12-08T11:44:21Z","updated_at":"2024-12-09T16:19:34Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Describe the issue:

I'm writing a loader for the npy file format in a non-Python language.  Part of the header of this file is a JSON encoded dictionary.  I am unable to parse it in my other language because the JSON numpy is generating does not conform to the JSON standard.  

https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/generated/numpy.lib.format.html

Specifically, the JSON standard requires:
- double quotes for string literals
- square brackets for arrays
- lower case for true and false boolean values

The .npy file header uses
- single quotes for string literals
- round brackets for arrays
- capitalized True and False for boolean values

Dictionary extracted from the .npy file I generated:
```
{'descr': '<f8', 'fortran_order': False, 'shape': (10, 50, 60, 70), }
```

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
import numpy as np
import opensimplex

feature_size = 10
size_x = 70
size_y = 60
size_z = 50
size_w = 10

ix = np.arange(0, feature_size, feature_size / size_x)
iy = np.arange(0, feature_size, feature_size / size_y)
iz = np.arange(0, feature_size, feature_size / size_z)
iw = np.arange(0, feature_size, feature_size / size_w)

arr = opensimplex.noise4array(ix, iy, iz, iw)
arr = (arr + 1) * .5

np.save('noise_data_4d.npy', arr)
```


### Error message:

_No response_

### Python and NumPy Versions:

2.1.3
3.11.3 (tags/v3.11.3:f3909b8, Apr  4 2023, 23:49:59) [MSC v.1934 64 bit (AMD64)]

### Runtime Environment:

_No response_

### Context for the issue:

The JSON dictionary should adhere to the JSON standard for compatibility with other programming languages.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27934/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27934/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2528546396","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/27934#issuecomment-2528546396","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27934","id":2528546396,"node_id":"IC_kwDOAA3dP86Wto5c","user":{"login":"ngoldbaum","id":3126246,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjMxMjYyNDY=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/3126246?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/ngoldbaum","html_url":"https://github.com/ngoldbaum","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ngoldbaum/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ngoldbaum/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ngoldbaum/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ngoldbaum/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ngoldbaum/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ngoldbaum/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ngoldbaum/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ngoldbaum/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ngoldbaum/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-12-09T16:19:32Z","updated_at":"2024-12-09T16:19:32Z","author_association":"MEMBER","body":"The trouble with changing any of these details is it will break naive parsers, and we don't want to do that. A little surprised to not find a pre-existing issue. If I just missed one please pointe me at it.

Right now the numpy Cython interface is more-or-less undocumented. Probably a symptom of being moved out of Cython into NumPy.

### Idea or request for content:

We should at least mention if not list everything that's in the cython `pxd` headers we ship, possibly along with usage examples if those make sense.

+
+
+def npystring_pack(arr):
+    cdef char *string = "Hello world"
+    cdef size_t size = 11
+
+    allocator = cnp.NpyString_acquire_allocator(
+        <cnp.PyArray_StringDTypeObject *>cnp.PyArray_DESCR(arr)
+    )
+
+    # copy string->packed_string, the pointer to the underlying array buffer
+    if cnp.NpyString_pack(
+        allocator, <cnp.npy_packed_static_string *>cnp.PyArray_DATA(arr), string, size,
+    ) == -1:
+        return Summary

This PR adds cython wrappers for the NpyString API, checking off [one of the items on the remaining NpyString API todo list](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/25693).

## Changes

- Exposed the NpyString API to cython. Opaque types in the C-API are kept opaque here.
- Added tests of the cython wrappers for packing strings into an vstring array and loading them from a vstring array, and for acquiring/releasing multiple allocators at a single time.

Summary

This PR adds cython wrappers for the NpyString API, checking off [one of the items on the remaining NpyString API todo list](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/25693).

## Changes

- Exposed the NpyString API to cython. Opaque types in the C-API are kept opaque here.
- Added tests of the cython wrappers for packing strings into an vstring array and loading them from a vstring array, and for acquiring/releasing multiple allocators at a single time.

cc @ngoldbaum - can you take a look?

Summary

This PR adds cython wrappers for the NpyString API, checking off [one of the items on the remaining NpyString API todo list](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/25693).

## Changes

- Exposed the NpyString API to cython. Opaque types in the C-API are kept opaque here.
- Added tests of the cython wrappers for packing strings into an vstring array and loading them from a vstring array, and for acquiring/releasing multiple allocators at a single time.

cc @ngoldbaum - can you take a look?

Summary

This PR adds cython wrappers for the NpyString API, checking off [one of the items on the remaining NpyString API todo list](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/25693).

## Changes

- Exposed the NpyString API to cython. Opaque types in the C-API are kept opaque here.
- Added tests of the cython wrappers for packing strings into an vstring array and loading them from a vstring array, and for acquiring/releasing multiple allocators at a single time.

cc @ngoldbaum - can you take a look?

Summary

This PR adds cython wrappers for the NpyString API, checking off [one of the items on the remaining NpyString API todo list](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/25693).

## Changes

- Exposed the NpyString API to cython. Opaque types in the C-API are kept opaque here.
- Added tests of the cython wrappers for packing strings into an vstring array and loading them from a vstring array, and for acquiring/releasing multiple allocators at a single time.

cc @ngoldbaum - can you take a look?

Numpy 2.1.3 works fine but with 2.2.0 the interpreter segfaults at exit.

Both Python 3.13.0t and 3.13.1t segfault at exit, both 3.13.0 and 3.13.1 do not.

I can reproduce this on macOS (amd64), Debian 11 (amd64) and Debian 12 (arm64). However also other combinations might be affected.

All the interpreters are built in debug mode with pyenv, ex. `pyenv install -g 3.13.1t`. ~Did not check if non-debug builds are affected as well.~ Python 3.13.1t non-debug (`pyenv install 3.13.1t`), at least on macOS, does not exhibit the segfault.

The discriminating Python build options seem to be:
1. debug mode
2. free threading

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
import numpy
```

Both Python 3.13.0t and 3.13.1t segfault at exit, both 3.13.0 and 3.13.1 do not.

I can reproduce this on macOS (amd64), Debian 11 (amd64) and Debian 12 (arm64). However also other combinations might be affected.

All the interpreters are built in debug mode with pyenv, ex. `pyenv install -g 3.13.1t`. ~Did not check if non-debug builds are affected as well.~ Python 3.13.1t non-debug (`pyenv install 3.13.1t`), at least on macOS, does not exhibit the segfault.

The discriminating Python build options seem to be:
1. debug mode
2. free threading

```python
import numpy
```

Numpy 2.1.3 works fine but with 2.2.0 the interpreter segfaults at exit.

Both Python 3.13.0t and 3.13.1t segfault at exit, both 3.13.0 and 3.13.1 do not.

I can reproduce this on macOS (amd64), Debian 11 (amd64) and Debian 12 (arm64). However also other combinations might be affected.

All the interpreters are built in debug mode with pyenv, ex. `pyenv install -g 3.13.1t`. ~Did not check if non-debug builds are affected as well.~ Python 3.13.1t non-debug (`pyenv install 3.13.1t`), at least on macOS, does not exhibit the segfault.

The discriminating Python build options seem to be:
1. debug mode
2. free threading

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
import numpy
```

All the interpreters are built in debug mode with pyenv, ex. `pyenv install -g 3.13.1t`. ~Did not check if non-debug builds are affected as well.~ Python 3.13.1t non-debug (`pyenv install 3.13.1t`), at least on macOS, does not exhibit the segfault.

The discriminating Python build options seem to be:
1. debug mode
2. free threading

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
import numpy
```

Documentation","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-12-11T16:36:38Z","updated_at":"2024-12-11T16:54:42Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Issue with current documentation:

I have built a number of packages against NumPy and looked at things like the [depending on NumPy](https://numpy.org/devdocs/dev/depending_on_numpy.html#understanding-numpy-s-versioning-and-api-abi-stability) docs. However, I don't see it mentioned anywhere under what circumstances you can safely build `abi3` wheels, if any (and I actually don't know the answer :facepalm: !). So it would be great to add a note about this explicitly to the docs.

### Idea or request for content:

Documentation","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":3,"created_at":"2024-12-11T16:36:38Z","updated_at":"2024-12-11T19:49:01Z","closed_at":"2024-12-11T19:49:01Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Issue with current documentation:

I have built a number of packages against NumPy and looked at things like the [depending on NumPy](https://numpy.org/devdocs/dev/depending_on_numpy.html#understanding-numpy-s-versioning-and-api-abi-stability) docs. However, I don't see it mentioned anywhere under what circumstances you can safely build `abi3` wheels, if any (and I actually don't know the answer :facepalm: !). So it would be great to add a note about this explicitly to the docs.

### Idea or request for content:

+@set_module("numpy.strings")
+def slice(a, start=None, stop=None, step=None, /):
+    """
+    Slice the strings in `a` by slices specified by `start`, `stop`, `step`.
+    Like in the regular Python `slice` object, if only `start` is
+    specified then it is interpreted as the `stop`.
+
+    Parameters
+    ----------
+    a : array-like, with ``StringDType``, ``bytes_``, or ``str_`` dtype
+
+    start : the start of the slice, can be None, an integer or
+            an array of integers which can be broadcasted to`a`'s shape
+
+    stop : the end point of the slice, can be None, and integer or
+           an array of integers which can be broadcasted to`a`'s shape
+
+    step : the step for the slice, can be None, an integer or
+           an array of integers which can be broadcasted to`a`'s shape
+
+    Returns
+    -------
+    out : ndarray
+        Output array of str or unicode, depending on input type
+
+    Examples
+    --------
+    >>> import numpy as np
+    >>> a = np.array(['hello', 'world'])
+    >>> np.char.slice(a, 2)
+    array(['he', 'wo'], dtype='<U5')
+
+    >>> np.char.slice(a, 1, 5, 2)
+    array(['el', 'ol'], dtype='<U5')
+
+    One can specify different start/stop/step for different array entries:
+
+    >>> np.strings.slice(a, np.array([1, 2]), np.array([4, 5]))
+    array(['ell', 'rld'], dtype='<U5')
+
+    Negative slices have the same meaning as in regular Python:
+
+    >>> b = np.array(['hello world', 'γεια σου κόσμε', '你好世界', '👋 🌍'],
+    ...              dtype=np.dtypes.StringDType())
+    >>> np.strings.slice(b, -2)
+    array(['hello wor', 'γεια σου κόσ', '你好', '👋'], dtype=StringDType())
+
+    >>> np.strings.slice(b, [3, -10, 2, -3], [-1, -2, -1, 3])
+    array(['lo worl', ' σου κόσ', '世', '👋 🌍'], dtype=StringDType())
+
+    >>> np.strings.slice(b, None, None, -1)
+    array(['dlrow olleh', 'εμσόκ υοσ αιεγ', '界世好你', '🌍 👋'],
+          dtype=StringDType())
+
+    """
+    # Just like in the construction of a regular slice object, if only start
+    # is speficied then start will become stop, see logic in slice_new.
+    if stop is None:
+        stop = start
+        start = None
+
+    # adjust start, stop, step to be integers, see logic in PySlice_Unpack

+    step = np.asanyarray(step)

+        raise TypeError(f"unsupported type {step.dtype} for operand 'step'")
+    if np.any(step == 0):
+        raise ValueError("slice step cannot be zero")
+

+    start = np.asanyarray(start)

+
+    if stop is None:

no need to call `asanyarray` or check dtypes if you create an array from `np.where`, I commit adds a `np.strings.slice` function which vectorizes slicing of string arrays. For example
```
    >>> a = np.array(['hello', 'world'])
    >>> np.char.slice(a, 2)
    array(['he', 'wo'], dtype='<U5')
```

This supports fixed-length and variable-length string dtypes. It also supports broadcasting the start, stop, step args:
```
    >>> b = np.array(['hello world', 'γεια σου κόσμε', '你好世界', '👋 🌍️'], dtype=np.dtypes.StringDType())
    >>> np.strings.slice(b, [3, 0, 2, 1], -1)
    array(['lo worl', 'γεια σου κόσμ', '世', ' 🌍'], dtype=StringDType())
```

Closes #8579

```
    >>> b = np.array(['hello world', 'γεια σου κόσμε', '你好世界', '👋 🌍️'], dtype=np.dtypes.StringDType())
    >>> np.strings.slice(b, [3, 0, 2, 1], -1)
    array(['lo worl', 'γεια σου κόσμ', '世', ' 🌍'], dtype=StringDType())
```

Closes #8579

gufunc","user":{"login":"ArvidJB","id":17654416,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE3NjU0NDE2","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/17654416?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArvidJB","html_url":"https://github.com/ArvidJB","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArvidJB/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArvidJB/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArvidJB/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArvidJB/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArvidJB/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArvidJB/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArvidJB/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArvidJB/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArvidJB/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"This commit adds a `np.strings.slice` function which vectorizes slicing of string arrays. For example
```
    >>> a = np.array(['hello', 'world'])
    >>> np.char.slice(a, 2)
    array(['he', 'wo'], dtype='<U5')
```

This supports fixed-length and variable-length string dtypes. It also supports broadcasting the start, stop, step args:
```
    >>> b = np.array(['hello world', 'γεια σου κόσμε', '你好世界', '👋 🌍️'], dtype=np.dtypes.StringDType())
    >>> np.strings.slice(b, [3, 0, 2, 1], -1)
    array(['lo worl', 'γεια σου κόσμ', '世', ' 🌍'], dtype=StringDType())
```

Closes #8579

gufunc","user":{"login":"ArvidJB","id":17654416,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE3NjU0NDE2","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/17654416?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArvidJB","html_url":"https://github.com/ArvidJB","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArvidJB/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArvidJB/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArvidJB/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArvidJB/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArvidJB/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArvidJB/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArvidJB/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArvidJB/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArvidJB/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"This commit adds a `np.strings.slice` function which vectorizes slicing of string arrays. For example
```
    >>> a = np.array(['hello', 'world'])
    >>> np.char.slice(a, 2)
    array(['he', 'wo'], dtype='<U5')
```

This supports fixed-length and variable-length string dtypes. It also supports broadcasting the start, stop, step args:
```
    >>> b = np.array(['hello world', 'γεια σου κόσμε', '你好世界', '👋 🌍️'], dtype=np.dtypes.StringDType())
    >>> np.strings.slice(b, [3, 0, 2, 1], -1)
    array(['lo worl', 'γεια σου κόσμ', '世', ' 🌍'], dtype=StringDType())
```

Closes #8579

gufunc","user":{"login":"ArvidJB","id":17654416,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE3NjU0NDE2","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/17654416?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArvidJB","html_url":"https://github.com/ArvidJB","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArvidJB/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArvidJB/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArvidJB/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArvidJB/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArvidJB/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArvidJB/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArvidJB/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArvidJB/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArvidJB/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"This commit adds a `np.strings.slice` function which vectorizes slicing of string arrays. For example
```
    >>> a = np.array(['hello', 'world'])
    >>> np.char.slice(a, 2)
    array(['he', 'wo'], dtype='<U5')
```

This supports fixed-length and variable-length string dtypes. It also supports broadcasting the start, stop, step args:
```
    >>> b = np.array(['hello world', 'γεια σου κόσμε', '你好世界', '👋 🌍️'], dtype=np.dtypes.StringDType())
    >>> np.strings.slice(b, [3, 0, 2, 1], -1)
    array(['lo worl', 'γεια σου κόσμ', '世', ' 🌍'], dtype=StringDType())
```

Closes #8579

gufunc","user":{"login":"ArvidJB","id":17654416,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE3NjU0NDE2","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/17654416?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArvidJB","html_url":"https://github.com/ArvidJB","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArvidJB/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArvidJB/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArvidJB/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArvidJB/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArvidJB/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArvidJB/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArvidJB/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArvidJB/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArvidJB/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"This commit adds a `np.strings.slice` function which vectorizes slicing of string arrays. For example
```
    >>> a = np.array(['hello', 'world'])
    >>> np.char.slice(a, 2)
    array(['he', 'wo'], dtype='<U5')
```

This supports fixed-length and variable-length string dtypes. It also supports broadcasting the start, stop, step args:
```
    >>> b = np.array(['hello world', 'γεια σου κόσμε', '你好世界', '👋 🌍️'], dtype=np.dtypes.StringDType())
    >>> np.strings.slice(b, [3, 0, 2, 1], -1)
    array(['lo worl', 'γεια σου κόσμ', '世', ' 🌍'], dtype=StringDType())
```

Closes #8579

":false}],"milestone":null,"comments":9,"created_at":"2024-11-18T17:20:30Z","updated_at":"2024-12-12T19:30:43Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/pulls/27789","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/27789","diff_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/27789.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/27789.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"This commit adds a `np.strings.slice` function which vectorizes slicing of string arrays. For example
```
    >>> a = np.array(['hello', 'world'])
    >>> np.char.slice(a, 2)
    array(['he', 'wo'], dtype='<U5')
```

This supports fixed-length and variable-length string dtypes. It also supports broadcasting the start, stop, step args:
```
    >>> b = np.array(['hello world', 'γεια σου κόσμε', '你好世界', '👋 🌍️'], dtype=np.dtypes.StringDType())
    >>> np.strings.slice(b, [3, 0, 2, 1], -1)
    array(['lo worl', 'γεια σου κόσμ', '世', ' 🌍'], dtype=StringDType())
```

Closes #8579

I don't think it makes sense to build and cache an index for the *whole* string, but there's no need to calculate the index of each character in the output string gufunc","user":{"login":"ArvidJB","id":17654416,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjE3NjU0NDE2","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/17654416?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArvidJB","html_url":"https://github.com/ArvidJB","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArvidJB/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArvidJB/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArvidJB/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArvidJB/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArvidJB/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArvidJB/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArvidJB/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArvidJB/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ArvidJB/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"body":"This commit adds a `np.strings.slice` function which vectorizes slicing of string arrays. For example
```
    >>> a = np.array(['hello', 'world'])
    >>> np.char.slice(a, 2)
    array(['he', 'wo'], dtype='<U5')
```

This supports fixed-length and variable-length string dtypes. It also supports broadcasting the start, stop, step args:
```
    >>> b = np.array(['hello world', 'γεια σου κόσμε', '你好世界', '👋 🌍️'], dtype=np.dtypes.StringDType())
    >>> np.strings.slice(b, [3, 0, 2, 1], -1)
    array(['lo worl', 'γεια σου κόσμ', '世', ' 🌍'], dtype=StringDType())
```

Closes #8579

Hi! I've been switching some code to the new `StringDType` and I came across a segfault when feeding `StringDType` arrays to lexsort. The example below is the same as in the documentation for lexsort: https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/generated/numpy.lexsort.html

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python3

import numpy as np
from numpy.dtypes import StringDType

# Works
surnames =    ('Hertz',    'Galilei', 'Hertz')
first_names = ('Heinrich', 'Galileo', 'Gustav')
ind = np.lexsort((first_names, surnames))
print(ind)

# Works
surnames2 = np.array(surnames, str)
first_names2 = np.array(first_names, str)
ind = np.lexsort((first_names2, surnames2))
print(ind)

# Segfaults
surnames2 = np.array(surnames, StringDType())
first_names2 = np.array(first_names, StringDType())
ind = np.lexsort((first_names2, surnames2))
print(ind)
```


### Error message:

_No response_

### Python and NumPy Versions:

```
2.2.0
3.12.8 (main, Dec  4 2024, 12:15:27) [GCC 14.2.0]
```

### Runtime Environment:

```

Summary

This PR adds cython wrappers for the NpyString API, checking off [one of the items on the remaining NpyString API todo list](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/25693).

## Changes

- Exposed the NpyString API to cython. Opaque types in the C-API are kept opaque here.
- Added tests of the cython wrappers for packing strings into an vstring array and loading them from a vstring array, and for acquiring/releasing multiple allocators at a single time.

cc @ngoldbaum - can you take a look?

+
+
+def npystring_pack(arr):
+    cdef char *string = "Hello world"
+    cdef size_t size = 11
+
+    allocator = cnp.NpyString_acquire_allocator(
+        <cnp.PyArray_StringDTypeObject *>cnp.PyArray_DESCR(arr)
+    )
+
+    # copy string->packed_string, the pointer to the underlying array buffer
+    if cnp.NpyString_pack(
+        allocator, <cnp.npy_packed_static_string *>cnp.PyArray_DATA(arr), string, size,
+    ) == -1:
+        return This PR adds cython wrappers for the NpyString API, checking off [one of the items on the remaining NpyString API todo list](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/25693).

## Changes

- Exposed the NpyString API to cython. Opaque types in the C-API are kept opaque here.
- Added tests of the cython wrappers for packing strings into an vstring array and loading them from a vstring array, and for acquiring/releasing multiple allocators at a single time.

cc @ngoldbaum - can you take a look?

Summary

This PR adds cython wrappers for the NpyString API, checking off [one of the items on the remaining NpyString API todo list](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/25693).

## Changes

- Exposed the NpyString API to cython. Opaque types in the C-API are kept opaque here.
- Added tests of the cython wrappers for packing strings into an vstring array and loading them from a vstring array, and for acquiring/releasing multiple allocators at a single time.

cc @ngoldbaum - can you take a look?

Summary

This PR adds cython wrappers for the NpyString API, checking off [one of the items on the remaining NpyString API todo list](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/25693).

## Changes

- Exposed the NpyString API to cython. Opaque types in the C-API are kept opaque here.
- Added tests of the cython wrappers for packing strings into an vstring array and loading them from a vstring array, and for acquiring/releasing multiple allocators at a single time.

cc @ngoldbaum - can you take a look?

It might sometimes be useful to define ad-hoc custom dtypes that give you runtime type checking and custom dispatch, all in Python without needing to use the C DType API.

This would work well for cases that object DType is already well-suited performance-wise, but where users would like type checking, custom semantics, casts, and ufunc implementations.

At a low level this would be object dtype and the scalars would all be instances of the same scalar type.

See [this discourse thread](https://discuss.python.org/t/math-exp-and-math-log-delegate-to-exp-and-log-methods/73495/92) where the complexity cliff involved in using the DType API led to this idea.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/28037/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/28037/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

a rude to directly ping people who aren't directly participating in a pull request discussion like you've done twice now in this PR.

The NumPy project has a slack you can join to ask questions like this. See [this page](https://numpy.org/devdocs/dev/index.html#contributing-to-numpy) in the docs for more details. In general I find it's best to ask questions about open source projects in public channels that are expressly for asking developer questions. Generally open source projects work under an asynchronous communication model unless you happen to be having a real-time chat with someone, so it may take some time before someone responds. It's OK to ping again after a while, but definitely not twice in one day.

If no one has time to answer your question then you might not get an answer. Helping people with these sorts of questions can be time consuming and also can drain social batteries.

To answer your question, I'd try running the tests passing the `--pdb` option to pytest:

```
spin test -- --pdb
```

which drops into a pdb debugger prompt when a test fails. s":10,"created_at":"2024-11-18T17:20:30Z","updated_at":"2024-12-25T15:15:48Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"draft":false,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/pulls/27789","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/27789","diff_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/27789.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/pull/27789.patch","merged_at":null},"body":"This commit adds a `np.strings.slice` function which vectorizes slicing of string arrays. For example
```
    >>> a = np.array(['hello', 'world'])
    >>> np.char.slice(a, 2)
    array(['he', 'wo'], dtype='<U5')
```

This supports fixed-length and variable-length string dtypes. It also supports broadcasting the start, stop, step args:
```
    >>> b = np.array(['hello world', 'γεια σου κόσμε', '你好世界', '👋 🌍️'], dtype=np.dtypes.StringDType())
    >>> np.strings.slice(b, [3, 0, 2, 1], -1)
    array(['lo worl', 'γεια σου κόσμ', '世', ' 🌍'], dtype=StringDType())
```

Closes #8579

For assignment using `__setitem__`, numpy is calling the `__array__` method on the value with a `copy=True`.  This can be seen by running the code example.

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
import numpy

class MyThing(object):
    def __array__(self, dtype=None, copy=None):
        print(f"MyThing.__array__(dtype={dtype}, copy={copy})")
        return numpy.ones((5, 5))

u = numpy.zeros((5, 5))
v = MyThing()

u[...] = v
```

_No response_

Bug","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"closed","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2024-09-18T00:44:36Z","updated_at":"2024-11-01T15:49:30Z","closed_at":"2024-11-01T15:49:30Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Describe the issue:

Here's a repro with the mpfdtype from https://github.com/numpy/numpy-user-dtypes/tree/cf6cf91571982cccb8098f8004962d0c2835bf56/mpfdtype
installed with `pip install .` in that directory of that repo.

```
In [1]: import mpfdtype, numpy as np

In [2]: np.dtype(mpfdtype.MPFloat)
Out[2]: dtype('O')
```

But that's not the correct answer, because `mpfdtype.MPFDtype` is a thing and registered with NumPy, with its `.typeobj` pointing to `mpfdtype.MPFloat`: https://github.com/numpy/numpy-user-dtypes/blob/cf6cf91571982cccb8098f8004962d0c2835bf56/mpfdtype/mpfdtype/src/dtype.c#L241

So the only reasonable answer would be the dtype object already known to numpy.

As best I can tell, `PyArray_DescrFromTypeObject` doesn't have any logic to look at the type objects of registered user dtypes:
https://github.com/numpy/numpy/blob/da79a56e6aa8aeafcdd218fd2e4c534e84ae62bb/numpy/_core/src/multiarray/scalarapi.c#L292

and it falls back to walking up the `__mro__` of the user dtype.

```
In [3]: mpfdtype.MPFloat.__mro__
Out[3]: (MPFDType.MPFDType, object)
```

which is how we end up with `dtype('O')`.

There is logic to resolve user dtypes in there, but it only works for legacy dtypes, as best I can tell.

```python
See above.
```

Also we have the github actions config for numpy-user-dtypes itself, so hopefully getting it set up on once CI job on gitnub actions isn't do hard.

The purpose of this is to make it easier to test the DType system itself. Right now NumPy includes a DType specifically for testing purposes called `_ScaledFloatTestDType` that's written using the new DType system. StringDType can also be used. But unfortunately both are somewhat unusual DTypes (notably, both use built-in Python types as scalar types) so it's sometimes awkward to write tests for the DType system.

If, as part of the NumPy test I could (optionally) import one or more of the DTypes in the user DType repo, it would be a lot easier to write tests.

An alternative is to add more internal test DTypes to NumPy, but I don't think there's a ton of appetite for that.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2452704462/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

Mailing list should usually be notified."}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":4,"created_at":"2024-10-23T10:49:04Z","updated_at":"2024-11-03T18:05:25Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Issue with current documentation:

Some C-API functions have a limit in the number of arguments that can handle, given by the macro  `NPY_MAXARGS`. But that limit is not described in the documentation of the function `NpyIter_MultiNew`. Other functions related with iterators might be affected, but you don't know it by reading the documentation.


### Idea or request for content:

don't see an issue for this yet, so I figured I'd open one.

Currently, you can do `pip install jupyter` and at least on my Mac development machine with compiler installed, you do eventually build and install a jupyter wheel. However:

```bash
goldbaum at Nathans-MBP in ~
○  python -VV
Python 3.13.0 experimental free-threading build (main, Oct 10 2024, 10:28:54) [Clang 16.0.0 (clang-1600.0.26.3)]

goldbaum at Nathans-MBP in ~
○  jupyter notebook
[1]    82211 segmentation fault  jupyter notebook
```

Unfortunately doing this in a Python launched under a C debugger doesn't give me a useful traceback from the segfault:

```
goldbaum at Nathans-MBP in ~
○  lldb $(pyenv which python) -- -m jupyter notebook
(lldb) target create "/Users/goldbaum/.pyenv/versions/3.13.0t/bin/python"
Current executable set to '/Users/goldbaum/.pyenv/versions/3.13.0t/bin/python' (arm64).
(lldb) settings set -- target.run-args  "-m" "jupyter" "notebook"
(lldb) r
Process 82671 launched: '/Users/goldbaum/.pyenv/versions/3.13.0t/bin/python' (arm64)
Process 82671 exited with status = 5 (0x00000005) Terminated due to signal 5
(lldb) bt
error: Command requires a process which is currently stopped.
```

Likely this is happening because one or more of Jupyter's dependencies don't support free-threading yet (e.g. CFFI).

It would be nice if this issue could be detected and surfaced earlier with a useful error message before the crash happens.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/jupyter/notebook/issues/7512/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/jupyter/notebook/issues/7512/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null}}

I think adding a github actions CI job that builds and installs numpy then builds and installs all the numpy-user-dtypes dtypes and runs their tests as well as the numpy tests is a good place to start. 

From there we can add one or more of the dtypes as optional dependencies for the test suite. I'd probably start with metadatadtype because it doesn't haven't any dependencies besides NumPy. 

Running the test suite should fail gracefully if the dtypes aren't installed so all the test jobs tests don't need to depend on GNU MPF or SLEEF being installed. 

The github actions CI job you set up in the first step should start automatically running new tests that depend on the dtypes being installed.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2457873967/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

This makes the MacOS and Linux tests use the forked version of `setup-python` to enable integrating running the free-threaded tests.

This creates two new CI jobs that run against every PR for MacOS free-threaded using builds on intel and ARM CPUs. The linux CI job isn't new, but now it's integrated better with the smoke tests. I also took the opportunity to drop `3.13-dev` from the build matrix.

Updating Windows CI is blocked on a `spin` release, see https://github.com/scientific-python/spin/pull/241.

See https://github.com/Quansight-Labs/free-threaded-compatibility/issues/107 for context.

This makes the MacOS and Linux tests use the forked version of `setup-python` to enable integrating the free-threaded tests with the rest of the CI configuration.

This creates two new CI jobs that run against every PR for MacOS free-threaded using builds on intel and ARM CPUs. The linux CI job isn't new, but now it's integrated better with the smoke tests. I also took the opportunity to drop `3.13-dev` from the build matrix.

Updating Windows CI is blocked on a `spin` release, see https://github.com/scientific-python/spin/pull/241.

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27704","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27704/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27704/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27704/events","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/27704","id":2634872615,"node_id":"I_kwDOAA3dP86dDPcn","number":27704,"title":"ENH:  Change the cannonical dtypes to reflect actual underlying dtype","user":{"login":"bashtage","id":5585221,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjU1ODUyMjE=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/5585221?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/bashtage","html_url":"https://github.com/bashtage","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bashtage/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bashtage/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bashtage/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bashtage/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bashtage/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bashtage/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bashtage/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bashtage/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/bashtage/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":6359990,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw2MzU5OTkw","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/labels/04%20-%20Documentation","name":"04 - Documentation","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":""}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2024-11-05T09:14:42Z","updated_at":"2024-11-07T02:00:32Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Proposed new feature or change:

If you use things like `np.double` this is an alias for `np.float64`, at least on every platform I've ever used NumPy on.  Meanwhile, the docs claim that [`np.float64` is an alias for `np.double`](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/reference/arrays.scalars.html#numpy.float64).

This creates issues when using automatic typing in Sphinx docs since Sphinx sees a type like `NDArray[np.double]` and resolves it as `NDArray[np.float64]`, but then cannot find `np.float64` since it does not appear in intersphinx. There are other types that have the same issue, 

A partial list that I have encountered:

* `np.cdouble` -> `np.complex128`
* All of the types of the form `np.intXX` and `np.uintXX` are reported as aliases of other C types, but really are the canonical type used on a platform.  This also is surprising since when working cross platform it is more natural to use `np.int64` rather than `np.intp`, which may differ. 

Ideally, these types would no longer be the aliases in the docs and would become stand-alone definitions that can be targetted in intersphinx.

That said, maybe in 2024 we can just assume stdint.h is available and define the sized integer dtypes in terms of that?

That said I agree it’s not helpful that these internal historical details ate exposed in the docs. It would be better to not worry about how the sized types are defined internally and just update all the docs to use the sized dtypes explicitly and move away from the C data types as much as possible.

{"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27709","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27709/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27709/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27709/events","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/27709","id":2639639540,"node_id":"I_kwDOAA3dP86dVbP0","number":27709,"title":"BUG: Use of PyErr_Occurred() with GIL released","user":{"login":"hawkinsp","id":348932,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjM0ODkzMg==","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/348932?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/hawkinsp","html_url":"https://github.com/hawkinsp","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hawkinsp/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hawkinsp/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hawkinsp/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hawkinsp/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hawkinsp/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hawkinsp/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hawkinsp/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hawkinsp/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/hawkinsp/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[{"id":6359214,"node_id":"MDU6TGFiZWw2MzU5MjE0","url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/labels/00%20-%20Bug","name":"00 - Bug","color":"fef2c0","default":false,"description":null}],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-11-07T02:00:35Z","updated_at":"2024-11-07T02:03:33Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Describe the issue:

`generic_wrapped_legacy_loop` calls `PyErr_Occurred`, which requires the GIL: https://github.com/numpy/numpy/blob/98464cc0cbc1f211482a0756ded305bed1599f18/numpy/_core/src/umath/legacy_array_method.c#L99

This function can be called from `PyUFunc_Accumulate` in a context where the GIL has been released: https://github.com/numpy/numpy/blob/98464cc0cbc1f211482a0756ded305bed1599f18/numpy/_core/src/umath/ufunc_object.c#L2925

We may release the GIL for a large array if https://github.com/numpy/numpy/blob/98464cc0cbc1f211482a0756ded305bed1599f18/numpy/_core/src/umath/ufunc_object.c#L2919C25-L2919C42 `PyDataType_REFCHK(descrs[0])` is true, but that's not a sufficient test: a legacy dtype, even those that do not require reference counting may lead to `generic_wrapped_legacy_loop` being called. In the repro below, this happens with `ml_dtypes.bfloat16`, from the `ml-dtypes` pip package.

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
In [1]: import numpy as np, ml_dtypes
In [2]: x = np.zeros((999999,), ml_dtypes.bfloat16)
In [3]: np.cumsum(x)
Segmentation fault: 11
```


### Error message:

```shell
Segfault. If Python is built with debugging enabled, then we received a `PyGILState_Check` error from `PyErr_Occurred`.
```


### Python and NumPy Versions:

2.1.3
3.12.3 (main, May 30 2024, 08:00:27) [Clang 15.0.0 (clang-1500.3.9.4)]


### Runtime Environment:

n/a

### Context for the issue:

Running TensorFlow's tests under NumPy {"action":"created","issue":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27701","repository_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy","labels_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27701/labels{/name}","comments_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27701/comments","events_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/27701/events","html_url":"https://github.com/numpy/numpy/issues/27701","id":2632809338,"node_id":"I_kwDOAA3dP86c7Xt6","number":27701,"title":"ENH: StringDType equivalent for bytes","user":{"login":"Conchylicultor","id":9047355,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjkwNDczNTU=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/9047355?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/Conchylicultor","html_url":"https://github.com/Conchylicultor","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Conchylicultor/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Conchylicultor/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Conchylicultor/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Conchylicultor/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Conchylicultor/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Conchylicultor/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Conchylicultor/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Conchylicultor/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/Conchylicultor/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":1,"created_at":"2024-11-04T13:23:03Z","updated_at":"2024-11-07T02:17:09Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"NONE","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"### Proposed new feature or change:

numpy 2.0 add a type for variable length `str`: `np.dtypes.StringDType()`. However it is strange there's not the equivalent for `bytes` (variable length bytes array).

Variable lenght bytes array are often encountered. For example in machine learning, many examples are stored as arrays of encoded images (`bytes`).

There's a `np.dtypes.BytesDType`, but the behavior seems to match the fixed-length array:

```python
x = np.asarray([b'aaa', b'bbbff'], dtype=np.dtypes.BytesDType)
x.dtype  # Is '|S5' so waste space
```

Also no need to support missing data for variable-length bytes (or maybe there is?).

I want to someday make StringDType support encodings, so maybe this could be `StringDType(encoding=None)` or something like that. I think abstracting the code to not assume UTF-8 everywhere would be nice and a good step towards getting this to work.

I’d also want to make people select an encoding when working with bytestrings as real strings. The NumPy legacy bytes dtype does work with the string ufuncs, but that was only because the bytes dtype is used to represent ASCII data (it’s really a Python2 ASCII string!). I’d ideally like to make you pick an encoding before passing arbitrary bytes to an operation and assuming it’s ASCII.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/numpy/numpy/issues/comments/2461177623/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

# $

# $

# %% [markdown]
# # Problema de turbina axial (examen 30-11-2016)
# 
# Se tiene un escalonamiento de repetición de turbina como el de la figura. La presión y temperatura estáticas de entrada al rotor son 36.8 bar y 428 K. En las condiciones de diseño el mach absoluto de salida estator vale 0.77. El fluido es un gas perfecto con γ
# =1.33 y R= 287 J/kgK. Se pide:

# %%
"""Declaración de constantes"""
P1 = 36.8
T1 = 428 
M1 = 0.77
gamma =1.33
R = 287
Cp=1005

# %% [markdown]
# a) Dibujar en el anverso de esta hoja los perfiles de los álabes, indicando cual es estátor y cuál rotor y hacia donde se movería el rotor. Dibujar las cascadas con una solidez de 1. Obtener el valor de u1 y u2. Razonar si el escalonamiento es óptimo.

# %%
c1 = M1 * np.sqrt(gamma*R*T1)
print(f'c1: {c1} m/s')
alfa1 =  np.deg2rad(55.4)
w1 = 176.8
beta1 = np.deg2rad(2)
u1 = 250

c2 = 164.6
alfa2 = np.deg2rad(12.4)
w2 = 250.6
beta2 = np.deg2rad(-50)
u2 = 227.5

c1u = c1 *np.sin(alfa1)
print('c1u',c1u)
c2u = c2 *np.sin(alfa2)
print('c2u',c2u)
W = c1u*u1 - c2u*u2
print('Trabajo W[J]', W)
resultado = (c1**2-c2**2)/2-(w1**2-w2**2)/2+(u1**2-u2**2)/2
print(resultado)
display(np.abs(W-resultado)/W *100)
print("\nSummary of all velocities and angles used:")
print(f"c1 = {c1:.2f} m/s")
print(f"alfa1 = {np.rad2deg(alfa1):.2f} degrees")
print(f"w1 = {w1:.2f} m/s")
print(f"beta1 = {np.rad2deg(beta1):.2f} degrees")
print(f"u1 = {u1:.2f} m/s")
print(f"c2 = {c2:.2f} m/s")
print(f"alfa2 = {np.rad2deg(alfa2):.2f} degrees")
print(f"w2 = {w2:.2f} m/s")
print(f"beta2 = {np.rad2deg(beta2):.2f} degrees")
print(f"u2 = {u2:.2f} m/s")


# %% [markdown]
# b) Estimar el trabajo, las pérdidas, el rendimiento y la relación de presiones total a total. Sabiendo que los coeficientes de pérdida de energía cinética en rotor y estator valen 5.63% y 5.11% respectivamente.

# %%
coeficiente_perdida_rotor = 5.63/100
coeficiente_perdida_estator = 5.11/100
perdidas_estator = coeficiente_perdida_estator * c1**2/2
perdidas_rotor = coeficiente_perdida_rotor * w2**2/2
display(perdidas_estator)
display(perdidas_rotor)
print(colored('Temperaturas:',"blue",attrs=["bold"]))
T1t = T1 + c1**2/(2*Cp)
print(f'T1t: {T1t} k')
T0t = T1t
print(f'T0t: {T0t} k')
c0 = c2 #Escalonamiento de repeticion
T0 = T0t - c0**2/(2*Cp)
print(f'T0: {T0} k')
T1tr = T1 + w1**2/(2*Cp)
print(f'T1tr: {T1tr} k')
T2r_prima = T1
print(f'T2r_prima: {T2r_prima} k')
T2t = T0t - W/Cp
print(f'T2t: {T2t} k')
T2 = T2t - c2**2/(2*Cp)
print(f'T2: {T2} k')
#velocidades isentrópicas, las necesitamos para calcular las temperaturas isentrópicas:
print(colored('velocidades isentrópicas, las necesitamos para calcular las temperaturas isentrópicas:', 'cyan'))
c1s = c1*np.sqrt(coeficiente_perdida_estator +1)
print(f'c1s: {c1s} m/s')
w2s = w2*np.sqrt(coeficiente_perdida_rotor +1)
print(f'w2s: {w2s} m/s')
T1s = T1t - c1s**2/(2*Cp)
print(f'T1s: {T1s} k')
T2tr = T2 + w2**2/(2*Cp)
print(f'T2tr: {T2tr} k')
T2s = T2rt - w2s**2/(2*Cp)
print(f'T2s: {T2s} k')

#Presiones
print(colored('Presiones',"green",attrs=["bold"]))
print(f"P1: {P1} bar")
P1s = P1
print(f"P1s: {P1s} bar")
P0t = P1s * (T1s/T0)**(gamma/(1-gamma))
print(f"P0t: {P0t} bar")
P0 = P0t * (T0t/T0)**(gamma/(1-gamma))
print(f"P0: {P0} bar")
P2s = P1 * (T1/T2s)**(gamma/(1-gamma))
print(f"P2s: {P2s} bar")
P2 = P2s
print(f"P2: {P2} bar")
P2t = P2 * (T2/T2t)**(gamma/(1-gamma))
print(f"P2t: {P2t} bar")

# %% [markdown]
# c) Calcular la altura de los álabes

# %%
gasto = 65 #kg/s
ratio = 0.1
densidad1 = (P1*1e5)/(R*T1)
print('densidad 1:', densidad1)
Dm1 = np.sqrt(gasto/(ratio*np.pi*densidad1*w1*np.cos(beta1)))
print('Dm1:', Dm1)
H1 = ratio * Dm1
print('H1',H1)

densidad2 = (P2*1e5)/(R*T2)
print('densidad 2:', densidad2)
Dm2 = np.sqrt(np.abs(gasto/(ratio*np.pi*(densidad2)*w2*np.cos(beta2))))
print('Dm2:', Dm2)
H2 = ratio * Dm2
print('H2',H2)
```

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
import numpy as np

x = np.asarray(['aaa', 'bbb'], dtype=np.dtypes.StringDType())

y = np.vectorize(lambda x: x)(x)

print(y.dtype)  # Is `U3` rather than `StringDType`
```

When trying to index and array of type StringDType with an array of any unsigned integer type it fails returning a MemoryError (strangely):

MemoryError: Failed to load string in StringDType getitem
MemoryError: String deallocation failed in clear loop

It does work when the index array is cast to int.

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
import numpy as np
str_arr = np.array(["a" * 25], dtype=np.dtypes.StringDType())
idx = np.array([0], dtype=np.uint8)
print(str_arr[idx.astype(int)])
print(str_arr[idx])
```

I can reproduce this.

> MemoryError (strangely)

This indicates somehow the array buffer and arena allocation have gone out of sync. Unfortunately there's a long tail of these we've had to deal with because we keep finding places in the NumPy codebase that make (incorrect) assumptions about the output and input dtypes being identical in casts. Describe the issue:

When trying to index and array of type StringDType with an array of any unsigned integer type it fails returning a MemoryError (strangely):

MemoryError: Failed to load string in StringDType getitem
MemoryError: String deallocation failed in clear loop

It does work when the index array is cast to int.

### Reproduce the code example:

```python
import numpy as np
str_arr = np.array(["a" * 25], dtype=np.dtypes.StringDType())
idx = np.array([0], dtype=np.uint8)
print(str_arr[idx.astype(int)])
print(str_arr[idx])
```


### Error message:

```shell
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "test.py", line 10, in <module>
    print(str_arr[idx])
  File "lib/python3.10/site-packages/numpy/_core/arrayprint.py", line 1692, in _array_str_implementation
    return array2string(a, max_line_width, precision, suppress_small, ' ', "")
  File "lib/python3.10/site-packages/numpy/_core/arrayprint.py", line 776, in array2string
    return _array2string(a, options, separator, prefix)
  File "lib/python3.10/site-packages/numpy/_core/arrayprint.py", line 547, in wrapper
    return f(self, *args, **kwargs)
  File "lib/python3.10/site-packages/numpy/_core/arrayprint.py", line 580, in _array2string
    lst = _formatArray(a, format_function, options['linewidth'],
  File "lib/python3.10/site-packages/numpy/_core/arrayprint.py", line 935, in _formatArray
    return recurser(index=(),
  File "lib/python3.10/site-packages/numpy/_core/arrayprint.py", line 896, in recurser
    word = recurser(index + (-1,), next_hanging_indent, next_width)
  File "lib/python3.10/site-packages/numpy/_core/arrayprint.py", line 839, in recurser
    return format_function(a[index])
MemoryError: Failed to load string in StringDType getitem
MemoryError: String deallocation failed in clear loop
```


### Python and NumPy Versions:

Python: 3.10.6 
Numpy: 2.1.3

Marked as draft because I have a couple questions:

* Changing the C API isn't strictly necessary since there's a `void *reserved2` field I can re-use for this. Any reason not to?
* You can do this now:

```
In [1]: np.add.my_attribute = 7

In [2]: np.add.__dict__
Out[2]: {'my_attribute': 7}
```

Is that a thing we want? This adds a new way to monkeypatch numpy. People probably already do that in other pure python bits, but now they can do it with ufuncs too. I guess the consenting adults principle applies though and I'd be inclined to allow Marked as draft because I have a couple questions:

* Changing the C API isn't strictly necessary since there's a `void *reserved2` field I can re-use for this. Any reason not to?
* You can do this now:

```
In [1]: np.add.my_attribute = 7

In [2]: np.add.__dict__
Out[2]: {'my_attribute': 7}
```

Is that a thing we want? This adds a new way to monkeypatch numpy. People probably already do that in other pure python bits, but now they can do it with ufuncs too. I guess the consenting adults principle applies though and I'd be inclined to allow spin","user":{"login":"ngoldbaum","id":3126246,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjMxMjYyNDY=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/3126246?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/ngoldbaum","html_url":"https://github.com/ngoldbaum","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ngoldbaum/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ngoldbaum/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ngoldbaum/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ngoldbaum/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ngoldbaum/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ngoldbaum/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ngoldbaum/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ngoldbaum/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ngoldbaum/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"labels":[],"state":"open","locked":false,"assignee":null,"assignees":[],"milestone":null,"comments":2,"created_at":"2024-10-18T16:21:47Z","updated_at":"2024-11-12T20:18:25Z","closed_at":null,"author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","active_lock_reason":null,"body":"NumPy [defines a `spin build` command](https://github.com/numpy/numpy/blob/dc862b43e3bca09dcf5c235c46b2c7573e2e4b80/.spin/cmds.py#L96), which has fallen out of sync from the upstream `meson.build` command it uses. Reading the click docs, it seems like the pattern NumPy is using with `ctx.forward` is frowned upon.

I think it would be better if spin provided factories for click commands to make it easier for projects to extend upstream commands without having to effectively re-implement their API.

See e.g. https://stackoverflow.com/questions/55584012/python-click-dependent-options-on-another-option.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scientific-python/spin/issues/242/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"timeline_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scientific-python/spin/issues/242/timeline","performed_via_github_app":null,"state_reason":null},"comment":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scientific-python/spin/issues/comments/2471499392","html_url":"https://github.com/scientific-python/spin/issues/242#issuecomment-2471499392","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scientific-python/spin/issues/242","id":2471499392,"node_id":"IC_kwDOIIbpT86TUBaA","user":{"login":"ngoldbaum","id":3126246,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjMxMjYyNDY=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/3126246?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/ngoldbaum","html_url":"https://github.com/ngoldbaum","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ngoldbaum/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ngoldbaum/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ngoldbaum/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ngoldbaum/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ngoldbaum/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ngoldbaum/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ngoldbaum/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ngoldbaum/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/ngoldbaum/received_events","type":"User","user_view_type":"public","site_admin":false},"created_at":"2024-11-12T20:18:24Z","updated_at":"2024-11-12T20:18:24Z","author_association":"CONTRIBUTOR","body":"NumPy would just use the upstream `spin.cmds.meson.build` command provided by spin, but it needs to add one keyword argument to `build`, `with_scipy_openblas`. So it defines a new command with the signature

```
def build(ctx, meson_args, with_scipy_openblas, jobs=None, clean=False, verbose=False, quiet=False, *args, **kwargs)
```

This is missing both `build_dir` and `gcov` as arguments that are in the upstream command. You might think that because NumPy's spin command accepts `**kwargs`, if someone passed in `gcov`, then click or spin would just forward the command, but that's not how it works:

```
goldbaum at Nathans-MBP in ~/Documents/numpy on fix-generic-fancy-index-cast!
± spin build --clean --gcov
Usage: spin build [OPTIONS] [MESON_ARGS]...
Try 'spin build --help' for help.

Error: No such option: --gcov
```

If you look at the docstring for numpy's `build` command, you'll also see that it's missing a bunch of stuff that's in spin's `spin.cmds.meson.build` command. 

So to update NumPy's `spin build` command to match the features provided by upstream, someone is going to need to manually update the `build` function and docstring in NumPy. Or more likely forget to update it, as has happened, particularly to the docstring.

I also found that I needed to patch NumPy's spin config in order for NumPy to benefit from #241, because without an explicit `--prefix` override in NumPy, click doesn't pass `--prefix` to spin's command.

Ultimately, as I understand it, this is because we use `ctx.forward`, which the `spin` docs advise against ("this pattern is generally discouraged"): https://click.palletsprojects.com/en/stable/advanced/#invoking-other-commands

IMO it would be better if spin itself could provide a command factory that accepts some sort of structure that defines a keyword argument and associated docstring, and then spin builds the command function and corresponding docstring as is needed. That would also allow better customization, for example if a built-in argument like `--gcov` doesn't make sense for some project.","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/scientific-python/spin/issues/comments/2471499392/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}
